story.title: Borrowed Names
story.description: When Amina helps curate a museum exhibition about her grandmother's migration, she discovers that identity resists the tidiness of captions and labels. Across two chapters, she learns to recognise the difference between representing someone and understanding them.
story.author: ReadWell

vocabulary.definition: the traditions, history, and culture passed down within families or communities over time
vocabulary.exampleSentence: The exhibition described the community's heritage as something worth preserving, though Amina felt the word left too much out.

vocabulary.definition: a group of people who have left their original homeland and now live scattered in different parts of the world
vocabulary.exampleSentence: The diaspora her grandmother belonged to had not moved as a group but as individuals, each navigating the same city without a shared map.

vocabulary.definition: the way someone or something is shown or described, especially in art, media, or public life
vocabulary.exampleSentence: Amina questioned whether three letters and a photograph could count as fair representation of an entire life.

vocabulary.definition: a fixed, oversimplified idea about what a particular type of person or group is like
vocabulary.exampleSentence: Nadia said a stereotype was not always a lie but a truth made too comfortable to challenge.

vocabulary.definition: a smaller group within a larger society, with its own distinct practices, beliefs, or identity
vocabulary.exampleSentence: Her grandmother said she belonged to a subculture the exhibition could not name properly, but she could make sure it did not explain her incorrectly.

vocabulary.definition: to gradually become part of a new community or culture by adopting its customs and way of life
vocabulary.exampleSentence: When Amina explained that the exhibition would show how communities had assimilated into city life, her grandmother repeated the word as if testing it.

vocabulary.definition: a group that is smaller in number than the main group in a society, often with different characteristics or less political power
vocabulary.exampleSentence: Amina asked how the exhibition could represent minority experience without reducing it to a comfortable simplification.

grammarFocus.explanation: Non-defining relative clauses add extra information about a noun without identifying which one is meant. They are always set off by commas and can be removed without changing the core meaning of the sentence. We use who for people, which for things or whole ideas, where for places, and whose for possession. They are especially common in formal and literary writing.
examples: The archive room, which smelled of old cardboard and cold stone, was nothing like the gallery space upstairs.
Ms Hargreaves, who had been overseeing the exhibition for four months, stood reviewing the floor plan.
She lifted a photograph of a woman in a pale headscarf, who was looking slightly away from the camera.
Her grandmother's comment stayed with her through dinner, which was not a comfortable meal.
formGuide: Put commas around the extra information in a non-defining relative clause.
Use who for people and which for things.
Do not use that in most non-defining relative clauses.
usageNotes: Use this structure when the listener already knows which person or thing you mean.
The clause adds extra detail but is not essential to identify the noun.
grammarFocus.contrastWith: Non-defining relative clauses add extra information, while defining relative clauses tell you exactly which person or thing is meant.
commonMistakes: Using that instead of who or which.
Writing the clause as if it were essential information.

content.text: The archive room, which smelled of old cardboard and cold stone, was nothing like the gallery space upstairs. Amina had expected bright lights and neatly labelled items; instead she found three folding tables covered in folders, unlabelled boxes, and unsorted photographs. Ms Hargreaves, who had been overseeing the exhibition for the past four months, stood at the far end reviewing the floor plan. 'The community section still needs a student perspective,' Ms Hargreaves said, spreading the plan across the nearest table. 'And frankly, Amina, you are the one here who understands what this material means to families like yours.'

grammarAnnotations.targetText: The archive room, which smelled of old cardboard and cold stone,
grammarAnnotations.note: non-defining relative clause: extra information, not identification

grammarAnnotations.targetText: Ms Hargreaves, who had been overseeing the exhibition for the past four months,
grammarAnnotations.note: non-defining relative clause about a person

content.text: The photographs were mostly black and white, though a few had been printed from digital copies. Women at street corners, men with luggage at dock gates, children in coats too large for them. Amina lifted an image of a woman in a pale headscarf, who was looking slightly away from the camera as if she understood a photograph was not quite the right container for what she was feeling. The caption read: Community arrival, 1968. Heritage preserved through family memory. The word heritage, she thought, was doing a great deal of work. It was not wrong. It simply left out everything that made the arrival real.

grammarAnnotations.targetText: a woman in a pale headscarf, who was looking slightly away from the camera
grammarAnnotations.note: non-defining relative clause: adds detail without identifying the noun

content.text: She found her grandmother's material in a smaller box near the window, where the morning light made the old paper curl at the corners. There were three letters, a programme from a ceremony Amina did not recognise, and a photograph of a young woman who could only be Nadia at twenty, standing beside a tree in what looked like a park. The diaspora the exhibition described as a unified movement had, in Nadia's case, begun with a borrowed coat and no knowledge of the city buses. Amina turned the word representation over in her mind as she held the programme, whose cover showed a building she had never managed to identify. How accurately could seven photographs and three captions represent one person's passage through a life?

grammarAnnotations.targetText: a smaller box near the window, where the morning light made the old paper curl at the corners.
the programme, whose cover showed a building she had never managed to identify.

content.text: That evening, Amina brought the photocopies home. Her grandmother, who had been reading at the kitchen table when Amina came in, took one look at the programme and set down her tea. 'They want to include this in the exhibition?' she said at last. Amina explained that the goal was to show how different communities had arrived in the city and begun to assimilate into daily life. Her grandmother's expression did not change, but something behind it did. 'Assimilate,' she repeated, as if testing whether the word would dissolve on her tongue. 'What they mean is that we stopped insisting quite so loudly. That is not the same thing at all.'

grammarAnnotations.targetText: Her grandmother, who had been reading at the kitchen table when Amina came in,
grammarAnnotations.note: non-defining relative clause: extra information about grandmother

content.text: The comment, which Amina had not been quick enough to answer at the time, remained with her through dinner. She had believed the exhibition was doing something valuable; now she was less certain. She raised the question of minority experience more carefully after the plates were cleared, asking whether there was a way the show could represent it without reducing it to a stereotype. Nadia put the photocopies back in order before answering. She said the difficulty with museum exhibitions was not dishonesty but tidiness. 'A stereotype,' she said, 'is not always a lie. It is a truth made too comfortable to challenge. People visit an exhibition and leave with the sense that they have understood something, which is sometimes worse than knowing nothing at all.'

grammarAnnotations.targetText: The comment, which Amina had not been quick enough to answer at the time,
grammarAnnotations.note: which refers to the idea in the previous paragraph

grammarAnnotations.targetText: leave with the sense that they have understood something, which is sometimes worse than knowing nothing at all.
grammarAnnotations.note: which comments on the whole preceding clause

content.text: After dinner, Amina asked whether Nadia would be willing to meet Ms Hargreaves and speak directly about the archive material. 'The woman is thoughtful and skilled at her work,' Nadia said at last. 'But she has spent her career telling other people's histories, which gives her a particular kind of confidence I find tiring.' 'Still. I will come.' She folded the photocopies and returned them to Amina. 'I belong to a subculture your exhibition cannot name properly. But I can make sure it does not explain me incorrectly. That is different from agreeing to be explained.'

grammarAnnotations.targetText: she has spent her career telling other people's histories, which gives her a particular kind of confidence I find tiring.
grammarAnnotations.note: which refers to a whole preceding idea

content.text: Walking to the bus stop later, Amina thought about the photograph of the woman looking sideways from the camera. The woman, whose name appeared in no caption, had understood something about the limits of images that was not yet in any exhibition plan. Amina had spent the afternoon believing the show would give those early arrivals a public voice; her grandmother had spent thirty years knowing the difference between a voice and a caption. She did not know yet how to repair the archive material. But she understood now that the most important part of the whole project, which had not been scheduled into any timeline, was the conversation she had just had over cold tea.

grammarAnnotations.targetText: The woman, whose name appeared in no caption,
the most important part of the whole project, which had not been scheduled into any timeline,
grammarAnnotations.note: remove this clause and the meaning is unchanged — that is the test

comprehension.question: What does Amina find when she arrives in the archive room?
options: A neatly organised collection of labelled items ready for the exhibition
Three folding tables covered in unsorted photographs, folders, and unlabelled boxes
A completed floor plan with all display panels already in place
Ms Hargreaves in the middle of a meeting with community representatives
comprehension.correctAnswer: Three folding tables covered in unsorted photographs, folders, and unlabelled boxes
comprehension.explanation: Paragraph 1 describes the archive room as containing folding tables covered in folders, unlabelled boxes, and a dense stack of photographs that nobody had yet sorted. It was nothing like the bright, neat gallery Amina had expected.

comprehension.question: Why does Amina think the word 'heritage' is 'doing a great deal of work' in the photograph caption?
options: Because the caption is too long and contains too many difficult technical terms
Because the word covers over everything that made the arrival feel real to the people who lived it
Because heritage is a word her grandmother finds particularly offensive
Because the caption uses the word to describe the wrong person in the photograph
comprehension.correctAnswer: Because the word covers over everything that made the arrival feel real to the people who lived it
comprehension.explanation: At the end of paragraph 2, Amina recognises that the caption's use of 'heritage' is not dishonest but leaves out all the specific, human detail that made the historical moment real. The word does too much work with too little precision.

comprehension.question: What is Nadia's objection to the word 'assimilate' as the exhibition uses it?
options: She thinks the word is grammatically incorrect in the context of the caption.
She believes assimilation was something she actively resisted and never experienced.
She argues the word presents gradual cultural loss as though it were a natural and willing process.
She is unfamiliar with the word and asks Amina to explain it before reacting.
comprehension.correctAnswer: She argues the word presents gradual cultural loss as though it were a natural and willing process.
comprehension.explanation: In paragraph 4, Nadia says 'what they mean is that we stopped insisting quite so loudly.' She objects to the way assimilation is framed as calm adaptation rather than a gradual silencing of difference.

comprehension.question: What does Nadia mean when she says a stereotype is 'a truth made too comfortable to challenge'?
options: That a stereotype is always accurate and therefore impossible to argue against
That stereotypes contain a grain of truth but flatten it into something that stops people asking deeper questions
That museum visitors prefer comfortable ideas and will not read more complex exhibition labels
That the community's history is genuinely too simple to require a more detailed explanation
comprehension.correctAnswer: That stereotypes contain a grain of truth but flatten it into something that stops people asking deeper questions
comprehension.explanation: Paragraph 5 shows Nadia making a careful distinction: a stereotype is not simply a lie but a partial truth presented with enough confidence to discourage further thought. The danger is in the comfort it produces.

comprehension.question: What does the word 'diaspora' in paragraph 3 most likely mean?
options: the official process of obtaining citizenship in a new country
a group of people scattered from their homeland and living in different parts of the world
the preserved cultural objects belonging to a single immigrant family
the formal name for a community exhibition about migration history
comprehension.correctAnswer: a group of people scattered from their homeland and living in different parts of the world
comprehension.explanation: Paragraph 3 contrasts 'the diaspora the exhibition described as a unified movement' with the individual reality of Nadia's arrival. The word refers to the dispersed community of people who have left a shared place of origin.

comprehension.question: On what terms does Nadia agree to meet Ms Hargreaves?
options: She agrees fully, saying she is impressed by the curator's professional reputation
She refuses, saying she does not want her story included in the exhibition at all
She agrees but makes clear she will prevent misrepresentation, not simply endorse the exhibition
She agrees only if Amina writes out all her responses for her in advance
comprehension.correctAnswer: She agrees but makes clear she will prevent misrepresentation, not simply endorse the exhibition
comprehension.explanation: In paragraph 6, Nadia says she can make sure the exhibition does not explain her incorrectly, and adds: 'That is different from agreeing to be explained.' She will attend not to approve the exhibition but to correct it where necessary.

grammarPractice.prompt: Choose the sentence with a non-defining relative clause.
grammarPractice.correctAnswer: The museum, which closes on Mondays, was full of visitors.
grammarPractice.explanation: Non-defining clauses add extra information and are separated by commas.
options: The museum which closes on Mondays was full of visitors.
The museum, which closes on Mondays, was full of visitors.
The museum, that closes on Mondays, was full of visitors.
The museum which, closes on Mondays, was full of visitors.

grammarPractice.prompt: Complete the sentence: Mr Rahman, ___ works with my aunt, donated the photographs.
grammarPractice.explanation: Use who for extra information about a person.

grammarPractice.prompt: Correct the sentence: My sister, that lives in Seoul, is visiting next week.
grammarPractice.correctAnswer: My sister, who lives in Seoul, is visiting next week.
grammarPractice.explanation: That is not normally used in non-defining relative clauses.

grammarPractice.prompt: Combine the sentences using a non-defining relative clause: Agnes Hartwell founded the library. She was a determined organiser.
grammarPractice.correctAnswer: Agnes Hartwell, who was a determined organiser, founded the library.
grammarPractice.explanation: Add the extra information between commas.

grammarPractice.prompt: Choose the sentence that adds extra information correctly.
grammarPractice.correctAnswer: The archive, which was closed for years, has reopened to the public.
grammarPractice.explanation: The clause is extra information, so commas and which are correct.
options: The archive which was closed for years has reopened to the public.
The archive, which was closed for years, has reopened to the public.
The archive, that was closed for years, has reopened to the public.
The archive which, was closed for years, has reopened to the public.

vocabulary.definition: a story or account of events, especially one shaped with a particular purpose or point of view
vocabulary.exampleSentence: Amina decided the problem was not the exhibition's facts but its narrative, which kept arriving at conclusions too quickly.

vocabulary.definition: a way of describing something by saying it is something else, used to suggest a resemblance or quality
vocabulary.exampleSentence: Her grandmother said migration was so often described through the metaphor of a journey, as though arrivals had always known their destination.

vocabulary.definition: the way elements are arranged in a piece of writing, art, or display to create a particular effect
vocabulary.exampleSentence: Ms Hargreaves had given considerable thought to the composition of each display panel, placing the oldest photographs at eye level to draw visitors in immediately.

vocabulary.definition: causing strong memories, feelings, or images in the person who sees or reads it
vocabulary.exampleSentence: The black-and-white photographs were evocative in a way the digital prints beside them were not.

vocabulary.definition: open to more than one interpretation; not having a single clear meaning
vocabulary.exampleSentence: Nadia's expression during Amina's speech was ambiguous enough that even Ms Hargreaves could not tell whether she was moved or simply patient.

vocabulary.definition: to explain or understand something in a particular way, especially when several readings are possible
vocabulary.exampleSentence: What Amina wanted was for visitors to interpret her grandmother's story rather than simply receive it as a finished lesson.

vocabulary.definition: to have a strong and meaningful effect on someone; to feel true or significant
vocabulary.exampleSentence: The last sentence of Amina's speech was the one that seemed to resonate most, because it asked a question rather than offered an answer.

grammarFocus.explanation: We use cleft sentences and inversion to give special emphasis to one part of a sentence. An it-cleft moves one element to the front: It was the photograph, not the caption, that mattered most. A wh-cleft uses what as the subject: What she needed was the truth. Inversion after a negative adverbial places the auxiliary before the subject: Never had she felt more clearly what she wanted to say. These structures appear naturally in formal speech, inner thought, and literary narrative.
examples: It was not the audience that made her pause but the ambiguous expression on her grandmother's face.
What Amina had finally written was different from anything she had shown Ms Hargreaves.
Never had Amina felt so precisely the distance between making something and releasing it.
Rarely did a museum display begin by admitting its own limits.
formGuide: Use a cleft sentence like what ... is ... to highlight one idea strongly.
Use inversion after negative or limiting adverbs such as rarely, never, or only later.
Keep the auxiliary before the subject in the inversion pattern.
usageNotes: Use these structures to make a formal or emphatic point.
They are useful when a normal sentence would sound too flat.
grammarFocus.contrastWith: These forms are marked and emphatic. A simple sentence communicates the idea, but the cleft or inversion structure places special focus on it.
commonMistakes: Forgetting inversion after only or rarely.
Building the cleft sentence without a complete second half.
Using these forms when the sentence actually needs plain word order.

content.text: The gallery looked different with guests. What had seemed like an arrangement of photographs and objects during the preparation week had become, in the forty minutes since the doors opened, something closer to a story being read in real time. Amina stood near the entrance watching people move through the panels and cases Ms Hargreaves had spent two months refining. Some visitors slowed at the evocative black-and-white photographs; others moved directly to the text blocks. Never had Amina felt so precisely the distance between making something and releasing it. Her grandmother, who had arrived early and taken a seat near the window, had not yet looked at the exhibition. She was watching the visitors instead.

grammarAnnotations.targetText: Never had Amina felt so precisely the distance between making something and releasing it.
grammarAnnotations.note: inversion after negative adverbial: Never + auxiliary + subject

content.text: Ms Hargreaves had asked Amina to say a few words about what the project meant to her. The speech, which Amina had rewritten four times, was folded in her jacket pocket. She had discarded an earlier draft that opened with the official narrative of her grandmother's arrival — it had read correctly but resonated with nobody she had shown it to. What she had finally written was different from anything she had shown Ms Hargreaves. It began with a missing address on the back of a letter, and it argued that the most honest thing the exhibition could do was acknowledge what it could not contain. She was not certain Ms Hargreaves would welcome that. She was quite certain her grandmother would.

grammarAnnotations.targetText: What she had finally written was different from anything she had shown Ms Hargreaves.
grammarAnnotations.note: wh-cleft: what-clause as subject, emphasises the contrast

content.text: The gallery light was warm and even in the way only careful planning produced. Amina moved through the room while she waited, stopping at the panel that included the photograph of the woman in the pale headscarf, which had been enlarged to fill most of the display space. The composition placed it beside a contemporary image of the same street photographed from the same angle. Amina looked at the two images together and felt the metaphor working and failing at the same time. Continuity was the right word for what had been framed, but not for what it was actually framing. The woman in the headscarf was not a symbol of continuity. She had simply been alive.

grammarAnnotations.targetText: the photograph of the woman in the pale headscarf, which had been enlarged to fill most of the display space.

content.text: When Ms Hargreaves introduced her, Amina stepped forward. It was not the audience that made her pause but the ambiguous expression on her grandmother's face, which she could not interpret from across the room. She set the speech on the table beside her. She spoke from her notes instead. She said that what the exhibition showed was real, documented, and carefully prepared. She said that what it could not show was the space between the documents: the borrowed coat, the wrong bus, the forty years that passed before anyone called it heritage. Rarely did a museum display begin by admitting its own limits, she added, and she was not claiming this one did. She wanted visitors to leave holding an ambiguous feeling rather than a satisfied one.

grammarAnnotations.targetText: It was not the audience that made her pause but the ambiguous expression on her grandmother's face,
grammarAnnotations.note: it-cleft: emphasises what caused the reaction

grammarAnnotations.targetText: Rarely did a museum display begin by admitting its own limits,

content.text: There was quiet when she finished. Ms Hargreaves smiled with a precision that suggested she was processing. Three visitors near the far wall exchanged a look that seemed to resonate: not quite discomfort, not quite recognition, but something between the two an honest metaphor could have produced. What Amina had needed, she understood now, was not approval but accuracy. Her grandmother had turned to look at the exhibition. She had stopped in front of the woman in the headscarf. Her expression was no longer ambiguous. It was private, and that felt more truthful than anything else the evening produced.

grammarAnnotations.targetText: What Amina had needed, she understood now, was not approval but accuracy.

content.text: Ms Hargreaves joined Amina near the back wall once the crowd began to thin. She said that what Amina had done with the speech had surprised her, and that surprise, in this case, was a positive thing. 'You made it harder,' she said, 'which I think was correct.' 'It was the framing, not the material, that needed to change. I should have seen that before tonight.' Amina said the existing panels were still worth preserving. 'Can you come back Thursday?' Ms Hargreaves asked. 'I would like to get your perspective included in the revision.' Amina said she would.

grammarAnnotations.targetText: It was the framing, not the material, that needed to change.
grammarAnnotations.note: it-cleft: emphasises which element required correction

content.text: After the guests had gone, Amina sat with her grandmother in the empty gallery while the cleaners worked in the corridor outside. The lights were still at full brightness, which made everything look more resolved than it felt. Her grandmother said only one thing about the speech: 'You did not explain me. You left room.' Amina had spent two weeks thinking about the exhibition's capacity to represent or misrepresent. What she had not anticipated was that the most important response would be those four words, offered without sentiment, resonating because they were carefully chosen. Her grandmother stood, straightened her coat, and said she was ready to go. Some things, Amina thought, resisted being interpreted into lessons. That was exactly the point.

grammarAnnotations.targetText: What she had not anticipated was that the most important response would be those four words,
grammarAnnotations.note: wh-cleft: what-clause emphasises the unexpected nature of the response

comprehension.question: Why does Amina set aside her written speech before she begins speaking?
options: Because Ms Hargreaves asks her to speak informally at the last moment
Because she sees her grandmother's ambiguous expression and decides to speak from her own understanding rather than a prepared text
Because she has accidentally brought the wrong version of the speech
Because the gallery is too noisy for her to read clearly
comprehension.correctAnswer: Because she sees her grandmother's ambiguous expression and decides to speak from her own understanding rather than a prepared text
comprehension.explanation: Paragraph 4 shows Amina pausing when she notices her grandmother's expression. She unfolds the speech, refolds it, and sets it aside. It was not the audience but that unreadable expression that prompted her to abandon the prepared text.

comprehension.question: What is the key difference between Amina's discarded draft and the speech she actually delivers?
options: The discarded draft removed all reference to her grandmother to keep the speech professional
The final version begins with a missing address and argues the exhibition should acknowledge what it cannot contain
The final version reads directly from the exhibition labels to give accurate historical context
The discarded draft was shorter and more emotionally direct than the version she delivers
comprehension.correctAnswer: The final version begins with a missing address and argues the exhibition should acknowledge what it cannot contain
comprehension.explanation: Paragraph 2 explains that the final version began not with heritage but with a missing address on the back of a letter, and argued that honest exhibition-making means admitting its own limits. This was very different from the earlier narrative draft.

comprehension.question: Which of the following is an example of an it-cleft sentence from the chapter?
options: Never had Amina felt so precisely the distance between making something and releasing it.
It was not the audience that made her pause but the ambiguous expression on her grandmother's face.
What she had finally written was different from anything she had shown Ms Hargreaves.
Rarely did a museum display begin by admitting its own limits.
comprehension.correctAnswer: It was not the audience that made her pause but the ambiguous expression on her grandmother's face.
comprehension.explanation: An it-cleft uses the structure 'It was X that/who...' to move one element into focus. Here the sentence emphasises what caused Amina's pause — not the audience, but her grandmother's expression. The other options show inversion or a wh-cleft.

comprehension.question: Why does Amina say she wants visitors to leave with 'an ambiguous feeling rather than a satisfied one'?
options: Because she believes the exhibition is poorly made and deserves a critical reception
Because she thinks genuine uncertainty is more honest than the neat understanding the exhibition might otherwise suggest
Because her grandmother has told her to make the speech deliberately confusing
Because she is trying to embarrass Ms Hargreaves publicly in front of the guests
comprehension.correctAnswer: Because she thinks genuine uncertainty is more honest than the neat understanding the exhibition might otherwise suggest
comprehension.explanation: This connects directly to her grandmother's warning in Chapter 1: people leave exhibitions feeling they have understood, which can be worse than knowing nothing. Amina wants visitors to feel productive discomfort rather than false resolution.

comprehension.question: What does the word 'evocative' in paragraph 1 most likely mean?
options: technically precise and historically verified
strongly calling up memories, feelings, or images in the viewer
digitally restored and therefore more accurate than originals
officially approved as authentic by a cultural institution
comprehension.correctAnswer: strongly calling up memories, feelings, or images in the viewer
comprehension.explanation: Paragraph 1 notes that some visitors slowed at the evocative black-and-white photographs while others moved on. The word describes the emotional and sensory power those older images had, in contrast to the less affecting digital prints.

comprehension.question: What does Nadia's response to the speech — 'You did not explain me. You left room.' — reveal about what she values?
options: That she wanted Amina to include more specific biographical facts about her arrival
That she is disappointed the speech did not defend her publicly against the exhibition's framing
That she valued being represented without being reduced to a single finished interpretation
That she feels the speech was too vague and that Amina should have rehearsed the written version
comprehension.correctAnswer: That she valued being represented without being reduced to a single finished interpretation
comprehension.explanation: Throughout both chapters Nadia has resisted simplification. Her final comment praises Amina for exactly that: speaking about her without turning her into a lesson or a symbol. Leaving room means allowing complexity to remain.

grammarPractice.correctAnswer: What the label leaves out is the human story.
grammarPractice.explanation: A what-cleft gives special emphasis to one part of the message.
options: The label leaves out what is the human story.
What the label leaves out is the human story.
What does the label leave out is the human story.
The human story is what the label leaves out it.

grammarPractice.prompt: Complete the sentence: Rarely ___ a single object change the mood of a room so quickly.
grammarPractice.explanation: After a negative adverb like rarely, use inversion with does.

grammarPractice.prompt: Correct the sentence: Only later I understood the full meaning.
grammarPractice.correctAnswer: Only later did I understand the full meaning.
grammarPractice.explanation: Use inversion after only + time expression.

grammarPractice.prompt: Rewrite as a cleft sentence: The missing name mattered most.
grammarPractice.correctAnswer: What mattered most was the missing name.
grammarPractice.explanation: Use a what-cleft to emphasise the important idea.

grammarPractice.prompt: Choose the sentence with correct inversion after a limiting phrase.
grammarPractice.correctAnswer: Only later did Nadia understand what the label omitted.
grammarPractice.explanation: After only later, the auxiliary comes before the subject.
options: Only later Nadia understood what the label omitted.
Only later did Nadia understand what the label omitted.
Only later did Nadia understood what the label omitted.
Only later Nadia did understand what the label omitted.
